Creating the bayesdir R package 1 Introduction

Creating the bayesdir R package Sheng Jiang 2026-05-13 1 Introduction This package implements bayesdir, Bayesian directional data analysis, including model 1, model 2. The documentation and package are both created using one simple command: litr::render("index.Rmd", output_format = litr::litr_gitbook()) ## install.packages("./bayesdir/", repos = NULL, type="source") This line is purely for testing (to be deleted later!). # Complete development workflow my_load <- function(){ litr::render("~/repos/bayesdir/index.Rmd", output_format = litr::litr_gitbook(minimal_eval = TRUE)) devtools::document("~/repos/bayesdir/bayesdir") # Update documentation devtools::load_all("~/repos/bayesdir/bayesdir") } my_load() # Step 2: Check the package (optional but recommended) devtools::check("~/repos/bayesdir/bayesdir", document = FALSE) # Step 3: Install the package locally for full installation devtools::install("~/repos/bayesdir/bayesdir") # Step 4: Load the installed package (optional, if you want to test installation) library(bayesdir) setwd("~/repos/bayesdir/") ## 1. Delete the auto-generated _main.Rmd file unlink("_main.Rmd") unlink("_bookdown_files", recursive = TRUE) if(!dir.create("bayesdir")){ unlink("bayesdir", recursive = TRUE) } ## 2. Re-run the render command litr::render( "index.Rmd", output_format = litr::litr_gitbook( minimal_eval = TRUE ) ) ## 3. Document and load the package devtools::document("bayesdir") devtools::load_all("bayesdir")

2 Package setup The DESCRIPTION file is created using this code. usethis::create_package( path = ".", fields = list( Package = params$package_name, Version = "0.0.0.9000", Title = "bayesdir", Description = "Bayesian dynamic network models", `Authors@R` = person( given = "Sheng", family = "Jiang", email = "jiangsheng@cuhk.edu.cn", role = c("aut", "cre") ) ) ) usethis::use_mit_license(copyright_holder = "Sheng Jiang") The following is what will show up when someone types package?bayesdir in the console. #' bayesdir #' #' This package implements the `bayesdir` method. #' #' @docType package This package will have some dependancies: library(tidyverse) ## Warning: package 'ggplot2' was built under R version 4.4.3 ##library(ggplot2) ##usethis::use_package("tidyverse", type = "depends") ## usethis::use_package("ggplot2") usethis::use_package("ggplot2", type = "depends") usethis::use_package("MASS", type = "depends") usethis::use_package("parallel", type = "depends") usethis::use_package("mvnfast", type = "depends") usethis::use_package("stats", type = "depends") usethis::use_package("matrixsampling", type = "depends") usethis::use_package("matrixNormal", type = "depends") usethis::use_package("pgdraw", type = "depends") usethis::use_package("tmvnsim", type = "depends") usethis::use_pipe(export=TRUE)

3 Helpers functions These are some helper functions that are needed for the posterior computation These functions are not documented for now. (TODO: do this). #' A helper function to print the progress of a simulation. Place directly at #' the beginning of the loop, before any computation happens. #' @param isim isim. #' @param nsim number of sim. #' @param type type of job you're running. Defaults to "simulation". #' @param lapsetime lapsed time, in seconds (by default). #' @param lapsetimeunit "second". #' @param start.time Start time, usually obtained using \code{Sys.time()} #' @param fill Whether or not to fill the line. #' @param beep Whether to beep when done. printprogress <- function(isim, nsim, type="simulation", lapsetime=NULL, lapsetimeunit="seconds", start.time=NULL, fill=FALSE, beep=FALSE){ ## If lapse time is present, then use it if(fill) cat(fill=TRUE) if(is.null(lapsetime) & is.null(start.time)){ cat("\r", type, " ", isim, "out of", nsim) } else { if(!is.null(start.time)){ if(isim == 1){ lapsetime = 0 remainingtime = "unknown" endtime = "unknown time" } else { lapsetime = round(difftime(Sys.time(), start.time, units = "secs"), 0) remainingtime = round(lapsetime * (nsim - (isim - 1)) / (isim - 1), 0) endtime = strftime((Sys.time() + remainingtime)) } } cat("\r", type, " ", isim, "out of", nsim, "with lapsed time", lapsetime, lapsetimeunit, "and remaining time", remainingtime, lapsetimeunit, "and will finish at", endtime, ".") if(beep & isim==nsim){beepr::beep()} } if(fill) cat(fill=TRUE) } #' Stable computation of the log-sum-exp function. #' #' @param x Numeric vector. logsumexp <- function(x){ m <- max(x) ret <- m + log(sum(exp(x - m))) return(ret) }

5 Predictive Resampler 5.1 The main sampler The main sampler is called run_predresamp(). #' Approximate Polya-Gamma sampler. #' #' If BayesLogit is installed, this uses BayesLogit::rpg(). #' Otherwise, it uses a truncated infinite-sum approximation. #' #' @param n Number of samples. #' @param h Shape parameter. #' @param z Location parameter. #' @param trunc Truncation level for approximation. Defaults to 80. rpg <- function(n, h, z, trunc = 80){ h <- rep(h, length.out = n) z <- rep(z, length.out = n) if(requireNamespace("BayesLogit", quietly = TRUE)){ return(as.numeric(BayesLogit::rpg(n, h, z))) } out <- numeric(n) m <- seq_len(trunc) for(i in seq_len(n)){ denom <- (m - 0.5)^2 + z[i]^2 / (4 * pi^2) g <- rgamma(trunc, shape = h[i], rate = 1) out[i] <- sum(g / denom) / (2 * pi^2) } return(out) } #' Approximate Polya-Gamma sampler. #' #' If BayesLogit is installed, this uses BayesLogit::rpg(). #' Otherwise, it uses a truncated infinite-sum approximation. #' #' @param n Number of samples. #' @param h Shape parameter. #' @param z Location parameter. #' @param trunc Truncation level for approximation. Defaults to 80. rpg2 <- function(n, h, z, trunc = 80){ h <- rep(h, length.out = n) z <- rep(z, length.out = n) if(requireNamespace("BayesLogit", quietly = TRUE)){ return(as.numeric(BayesLogit::rpg(n, h, z))) } out <- numeric(n) m <- seq_len(trunc) for(i in seq_len(n)){ denom <- (m - 0.5)^2 + z[i]^2 / (4 * pi^2) g <- rgamma(trunc, shape = h[i], rate = 1) out[i] <- sum(g / denom) / (2 * pi^2) } return(out) } #' Approximate Polya-Gamma sampler. #' #' If BayesLogit is installed, this uses BayesLogit::rpg(). #' Otherwise, it uses a truncated infinite-sum approximation. #' #' @param n Number of samples. #' @param h Shape parameter. #' @param z Location parameter. #' @param trunc Truncation level for approximation. Defaults to 80. rpg3 <- function(n, h, z, trunc = 80){ h <- rep(h, length.out = n) z <- rep(z, length.out = n) if(requireNamespace("BayesLogit", quietly = TRUE)){ return(as.numeric(BayesLogit::rpg(n, h, z))) } out <- numeric(n) m <- seq_len(trunc) for(i in seq_len(n)){ denom <- (m - 0.5)^2 + z[i]^2 / (4 * pi^2) g <- rgamma(trunc, shape = h[i], rate = 1) out[i] <- sum(g / denom) / (2 * pi^2) } return(out) } #' Approximate Polya-Gamma sampler. #' #' If BayesLogit is installed, this uses BayesLogit::rpg(). #' Otherwise, it uses a truncated infinite-sum approximation. #' #' @param n Number of samples. #' @param h Shape parameter. #' @param z Location parameter. #' @param trunc Truncation level for approximation. Defaults to 80. rpg4 <- function(n, h, z, trunc = 80){ h <- rep(h, length.out = n) z <- rep(z, length.out = n) if(requireNamespace("BayesLogit", quietly = TRUE)){ return(as.numeric(BayesLogit::rpg(n, h, z))) } out <- numeric(n) m <- seq_len(trunc) for(i in seq_len(n)){ denom <- (m - 0.5)^2 + z[i]^2 / (4 * pi^2) g <- rgamma(trunc, shape = h[i], rate = 1) out[i] <- sum(g / denom) / (2 * pi^2) } return(out) }
6 Simulation Study: Bayesian vMF models for current direction Data 6.1 Sythetic data for illustration 6.2 Bayesian Inference on a conjugate prior. 6.3 Predictive Posterior via Martingale.

6 Simulation Study: Bayesian vMF models for current direction Data This vignette demonstrates a complete simulation study using bayesdir on synthetic traffic data from a grid road network. 6.1 Sythetic data for illustration 6.2 Bayesian Inference on a conjugate prior. Used as an benchmark. See Forbes & Mardia (2015) by \(\texttt{circglmbayes}\) Here \(\mu \in [0,2\pi]\) as an angle. \(\kappa \geq0\) as concentration. 6.3 Predictive Posterior via Martingale. Score based martingale sampling without pre-conditioner (inverse info). For efficiency, hybrid trick is adopted. sim_martingale <- function(n, n_sim = 100, mu_true = 2, kappa_true = 4, B = 500, M = 50, seed = 1234) { A <- function(k) ifelse(k < 1e-6, 0, besselI(k, 1, expon.scaled = TRUE) / besselI(k, 0, expon.scaled = TRUE)) score_fn <- function(eta, x) { k <- sqrt(sum(eta^2)) if (k < 1e-6) return(x) expected <- A(k) * eta / k x - expected } rvMF2 <- function(eta) { k <- sqrt(sum(eta^2)) if (k < 1e-6) { angle <- runif(1, 0, 2*pi) } else { mu_angle <- atan2(eta[2], eta[1]) angle <- as.numeric(rvonmises(1, circular(mu_angle), k)) } c(cos(angle), sin(angle)) } Z_list <- list() Tn_vec <- c() times <- numeric(n_sim) set.seed(seed) for (i in 1:n_sim) { theta_data <- as.numeric(rvonmises(n, circular(mu_true), kappa_true)) C <- sum(cos(theta_data)); S <- sum(sin(theta_data)) R <- sqrt(C^2 + S^2); Rbar <- R/n mu_hat <- atan2(S, C) if (Rbar < 0.001) kappa_hat <- 0 else if (Rbar > 0.999) kappa_hat <- 1/(2*(1-Rbar)) else kappa_hat <- uniroot(function(k) A(k) - Rbar, c(0.001, 100))$root eta0 <- kappa_hat * c(cos(mu_hat), sin(mu_hat)) Z_mat <- matrix(0, B, 2) T_vec <- numeric(B) t0 <- proc.time()[3] # for (b in 1:B) { eta <- eta0 V_mat <- matrix(0, 2, 2) ## General iteration for (t in 1:M) { x_new <- rvMF2(eta) s <- score_fn(eta, x_new) wt <- 1/(t + n) V_mat <- V_mat + wt^2 * (s %*% t(s)) eta <- eta + wt * s } ## hybird step w_cur <- sum(1 / ((1:M) + n)^2) w_rem <- sum(1 / ((M+1):(M+10000) + n)^2) Sigma_hat <- V_mat * (1 + w_rem / w_cur) eig <- eigen(Sigma_hat, symmetric = TRUE) eig$values[eig$values < 0] <- 0 inv_sqrt_Sigma <- eig$vectors %*% diag(1/sqrt(pmax(eig$values, 1e-12))) %*% t(eig$vectors) eps <- rnorm(2) eta_final <- eta + eig$vectors %*% diag(sqrt(eig$values * w_rem / (w_cur + w_rem))) %*% eps ## Calculate each stat for UQ. delta <- eta_final - eta0 Z_vec <- inv_sqrt_Sigma %*% delta Z_mat[b, ] <- Z_vec T_vec[b] <- sum(Z_vec^2) # For rough test, no rigirous proof. } t1 <- proc.time()[3] times[i] <- t1 - t0 Z_list[[i]] <- Z_mat Tn_vec <- c(Tn_vec, T_vec) } Z_all <- do.call(rbind, Z_list) colnames(Z_all) <- c("Z1", "Z2") cover_Z1 <- mean(abs(Z_all[,1]) <= qnorm(0.975)) cover_Z2 <- mean(abs(Z_all[,2]) <= qnorm(0.975)) cover_Tn <- mean(Tn_vec <= qchisq(0.95, df = 2)) list( Z = Z_all, Tn = Tn_vec, summary = data.frame( Coverage_Z1 = cover_Z1, Coverage_Z2 = cover_Z2, Coverage_Tn = cover_Tn, Mean_Time = mean(times), stringsAsFactors = FALSE ) ) } Note here for simplicity, I only test on with 500 chains res_mart10 <- sim_martingale(n = 10, n_sim = 100, B = 500, M = 50) res_mart30 <- sim_martingale(n = 30, n_sim = 100, B = 500, M = 50) res_mart30 <- sim_martingale(n = 100, n_sim = 100, B = 500, M = 50) result_table <- rbind(res_mart10, res_mart30, res_mart100) profvis::profvis(res_mart30 <- sim_martingale(n = 30, n_sim = 100, B = 500, M = 50) )
